name: Magnesium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Magnesium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power range for Magnesium oxide layer removal

wavelength.description: Optimal wavelength considering Magnesium's optical properties

spotSize.description: Optimal beam spot diameter for Magnesium surface treatment

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient Magnesium cleaning process

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Magnesium surface cleaning

pulseWidth.description: Optimal pulse duration for controlled ablation of Magnesium oxides

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Magnesium surface cleaning

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for thorough Magnesium cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal pulse overlap for complete Magnesium surface coverage

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of magnesium alloys to avoid melting (low melting point ~650°C); starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited to prevent substrate damage due to high reflectivity at 1064 nm. Source: Studies on Mg alloy cleaning (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018, Vol. 30, IPG Photonics specs for Yb-fiber lasers).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy rust/oils, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light contaminants. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on Mg to minimize heat accumulation. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and Adapt Laser manuals for metal surface prep.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for Mg to ablate contaminants with minimal HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces thermal effects but increases cost. 50 ns recommended for low reflectivity materials like Mg. Source: Laser physics research (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020, Vol. 132, on pulse duration effects in metal cleaning).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed fiber lasers in cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat buildup on thermally conductive Mg. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: >80 kHz may cause melting. Source: Academic literature (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019, Vol. 475, on rep rate optimization for light metals).

energy_density.description: Fluence below Mg damage threshold (~5 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe cleaning; 0.5-2 J/cm² for light contaminants, up to 4 for heavy. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² avoids ablation of substrate. Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017, Vol. 50, on IR laser interaction with Mg).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-200 μm) for fine detail on Mg surfaces, larger (300-500 μm) for broad area prep. 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning without excessive heat concentration. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems for metals.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on Mg to prevent overheating per pass. Recommended 2 passes for industrial removal. Source: Industry case studies (e.g., SPIE Proc. 2021, Vol. 11660, on multi-pass cleaning of alloys).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or overexposure; 30-50% for large areas, 60-80% for detailed Mg cleaning. 60% recommended to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Laser Technol. 2022, Vol. 145, on scan overlap in laser cleaning).
